# Go Reflection Module

## Overview
Go reflection (reflect package) provides runtime type inspection and manipulation capabilities.

## Files

### basics.mdx
- **Reflection Basics**: Introduction to Go reflection, three laws of reflection, Type vs Value, Kind types
- Key concepts: reflect.TypeOf, reflect.ValueOf, CanSet, CanAddr
- Use cases: generic functions, deep equality, dynamic slice creation
- Performance considerations: reflection is 10-100x slower than direct calls

### type-reflection.mdx
- **Type Reflection**: reflect.Type interface and type information queries
- Type properties: Name, Kind, PkgPath, Size, Elem, Key, Len
- Struct type: field iteration, struct tags, embedded fields
- Function type: input/output parameters, variadic functions
- Interface type: method information, Implements check
- Type operations: AssignableTo, ConvertibleTo, type equality

### value-reflection.mdx
- **Value Reflection**: reflect.Value for reading and modifying values
- Reading values: basic types (Int, Uint, Float, Bool, String), composite types (arrays, slices, maps, channels, structs)
- Modifying values: CanSet check, modifying basic types, composite types, struct fields
- Creating values: MakeSlice, MakeMap, MakeChan, New for structs
- Value operations: Compare, DeepEqual, IsZero

### struct-tags.mdx
- **Struct Tags**: Metadata mechanism for struct fields
- Tag syntax: `key:"value"`, multiple tags, options
- Reading tags: Tag.Get(), Tag.Lookup()
- Parsing tags: parsing options, complex tag formats
- Common applications: JSON/XML serialization, database mapping (GORM), validation
- Custom tag processing and best practices

### dynamic-calls.mdx
- **Dynamic Method/Function Calls**: Runtime invocation
- Method calls: MethodByName, Call, CallSlice for variadic methods
- Function calls: calling functions dynamically, CallSlice
- Interface method calls: dynamic interface method invocation
- Generic frameworks: RPC framework, event handlers, command dispatchers
- Performance optimization: caching method information

### best-practices.mdx
- **Best Practices**: When and how to use reflection properly
- When to use: serialization, dependency injection, ORM, deep comparison, generic functions
- When NOT to use: known types, hot paths, simple cases (use type assertions)
- Performance optimization: cache reflection info, code generation
- Error handling: proper error checking, panic recovery
- Common pitfalls: unexported fields, addressability, circular references
- Design patterns: adapter pattern, plugin system
- Testing: unit tests, benchmarks

## Related Topics
- Go interfaces (../interface/): Interface design and type assertions
- Go functions and methods (../functions-methods/): Method receivers, function types
- Go data types (../data-types/): Type system, composite types
